title: Midday Session Drop for Every Remote User After a 'Rename-Only' Terraform PR

ticket.summary: At 13:05 every remote user's VPN session dropped simultaneously and took ~4 minutes to recover. The only change in flight was a Terraform PR described as 'refactor: rename firewall NAT resource for naming-standard compliance — no functional change'. The apply job ran at 13:04. Firewall vendor support found no fault on the device.

topology.description: The perimeter firewall's NAT and VPN configuration is managed by Terraform through the vendor provider. The remote-access VPN's NAT exemption object is a Terraform resource; destroying it drops all established VPN sessions. Terraform tracks resources by their address (type + name) in state — renaming a resource without telling Terraform it moved makes it plan a destroy of the old address and a create of the new one.

logs.content:
13:04:41 terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
13:04:52 fw_nat_rule.vpn_exempt: Destroying...
13:04:55 fw_nat_rule.vpn_exempt: Destruction complete after 3s
13:04:56 fw_nat_rule.ra_vpn_nat_exemption: Creating...
13:08:31 fw_nat_rule.ra_vpn_nat_exemption: Creation complete after 3m35s

13:04:55 NAT rule 'vpn-exempt' deleted by api-svc-terraform
13:04:56 813 VPN sessions torn down (NAT exemption removed)
13:08:31 NAT rule 're-created' by api-svc-terraform

monitoring.notes: Session count graph: steady ~800 sessions, cliff to 0 at 13:04:55 (the exact second the destroy completed), slow ramp back after 13:08:31 when the replacement rule finished creating. The plan output in CI clearly showed '1 to add, 1 to destroy' — a destroy/create pair for what the PR claimed was a rename — but the job auto-approved the plan without a human gate.

hints: The PR claimed 'no functional change', but the plan said '1 to add, 1 to destroy' — in Terraform, what does renaming a resource do to state if you don't declare the rename?
Terraform tracks resources by address; a renamed address is a new resource. Look for a 'moved' block in the diff.

Without a 'moved { from = fw_nat_rule.vpn_exempt, to = fw_nat_rule.ra_vpn_nat_exemption }' block (or 'terraform state mv'), Terraform destroyed the NAT exemption and recreated it — dropping all 813 VPN sessions during the gap. Add moved blocks for renames and require human review when a plan shows any destroy.

solution.root_cause: PR #412 renamed the Terraform resource address (fw_nat_rule.vpn_exempt -> fw_nat_rule.ra_vpn_nat_exemption) without a 'moved' block or 'terraform state mv'. Terraform tracks resources by address, so it planned — and CI auto-approved — a destroy of the old resource and a create of the new one. Destroying the NAT exemption rule tore down all ~813 remote-access VPN sessions at 13:04:55; service only recovered when the replacement rule finished creating at 13:08:31.
solution.fix: For renames, declare the move so state follows the new address: add a 'moved { from = ..., to = ... }' block (or run 'terraform state mv') so the plan shows zero destroys; and change the pipeline so any plan containing a destroy requires explicit human approval instead of -auto-approve.
